advantages: Provide a principled, comparable measure of vector magnitude across dimensions and coordinate choices (for rotation-invariant L2 geometry).
Enable defining distances and similarity, which are foundational for clustering, retrieval, classification margins and loss functions.
Different norms encode different inductive biases—L1 sparsity, L2 smooth shrinkage—giving practitioners tunable regularization.
Norms support convergence analysis and stability tools (e.g. bounding gradient norms) with clear geometric meaning.
disadvantages: No single norm is universally best; a poor choice (e.g. L2 on unscaled heterogeneous features) can mis-rank neighbors and hurt models.
High-dimensional distances often concentrate (curse of dimensionality), making naive L2 less discriminative without careful scaling or dimensionality reduction.
L1 and L∞ are not rotation-invariant in the same way as L2, which can be undesirable when feature axes have no natural meaning.
Computing pairwise distances at scale is O(n²) in the number of points unless approximated with indexes or hashing.
